and your urban. When you first move to
South Korea, everything feels like an
adventure. The sights, the sounds, and
of course, the food. But what happens
when a novelty wears off and the reality
of daily life sets in? A holiday isn't
the same as building a life. So today,
I'm not going to give you the typical
tourist highlights. Instead, I'm going
to give you a reality check on what it's
like living as a foreigner in South
Korea. Now, disclaimer. This is based on
my personal experience living overseas
for over a decade, including Asia and
South Korea. And my friends who have
worked in Seoul for years, so I may
overgeneralize. Everyone will have their
own experience. So, if you'd like to add
something or think I got something
wrong, mention it in the comments. But
before you pack your bags, let's dive
into reality that hits the hardest.
Firstly, how big a deal is the language?
especially if you're planning on living
there like I did long term. So, let's
talk about the Korean language. You'll
often hear that you can get by in Korea
with English just fine. And for holiday,
yeah, that's true. But when it comes to
living there long term, building a
career, or having those deep and
meaningful relationships, you need to
understand Korean is not optional. It is
essential outside of teaching English or
very specific roles in international
firms like tech or finance. Many jobs
will require at least intermediate
Korean. And it's not just work. When
socializing and trying to make Korean
friends, it's a difference between being
on the outside versus being on the
inside. Without the language, you miss
the jokes, the cultural nuances, and the
everyday interactions that bond people
and make you feel included. You're
basically locked out of the true
authentic side of South Korea. Think
being at a market stall and struggling
to order food. In fact, I remember being
at aa trying to order a coffee and I
just didn't understand the English I was
saying. It was really frustrating and
very awkward. So, to get the most out of
South Korea, make sure you're actively
learning Korean. Use flashcards to
memorize new words. Speak Korean in your
daily life, even if it's really awkward.
And use apps like Hello Talk to meet
language partners. Learning a language
is really one day at a time. If you did
just 30 minutes every day after a month,
you'd be amazed at your own progress.
So, learning Korean opens doors, but
what about the people behind them? Next,
we're going to look at the social side,
making friends, dating, and why it's
easy to fall into the expat trap. As a
foreigner, you can be a real novelty to
Koreans. Seemingly boring or trivial
things back home can be really
interesting to them. I remember going to
an in-person meetup event in Seoul and
seeing groups of Koreans surrounding
each and every Westerner. It could make
you feel like a celebrity. Although for
me it did feel a bit uncomfortable.
Then there are the Korean cultural
dynamics like male, female, younger,
older or other outdated norms that some
Koreans find stifling or just dislike.
That means as a foreigner you can come
across as a refreshing blank slate with
no baggage and you can be really
appealing to those people. But that can
also be a barrier. It can be easy to
make many casual acquaintances but much
harder to make those deep lasting
friendships. If you don't understand,
immerse and even adapt to some clean
ways. There will always be a gap and
make it much harder to build those
friendships. On the other hand, it's
easy to latch on to other foreigners as
a social crutch. After all, you speak
the same language, you have the same
cultural norms, and you're all going
through the expat experience in Korea
together. So, it can be very easy to
spend every evening at your favorite pub
until the early morning, but these
relationships can be equally
superficial. Now, this doubly applies to
dating. I could make a whole video on
this, but I'll keep it short. If you're
dating a Korean, the differences in your
cultures can be really exciting and
refreshing at first, but that doesn't
translate to a real and meaningful
connection. And as relationship gets
more serious, the lack of common values
and the challenges of Korean culture,
wedding, and families can make it really
clear you two aren't on the same page.
So, what's the solution to the
challenges of making friends and dating
in South Korea? Well, it's really
simple. It means prioritizing people
that you share common values with and
that support you through your ups and
downs. Doesn't matter where they're from
or what language they speak. And those
common values can be anything from
sports to volunteering to life
aspirations.
And it also means adapting to some parts
of Korean culture, being flexible and
being thoughtful as well. So for me,
that meant spending less time at the pub
with foreigners, which is always the
easiest option for me, and spending more
time sober in soul, exploring art
galleries, having a great coffee, or
sharing a meal with people on a similar
wavelength. I was fortunate to make many
good friends in Korea, whether locals or
foreigners. Building those connections
takes effort. But hey, at least the
food's perfect, right? Well, let's get
to the dining scene beyond the Instagram
hype and explore what eating in Korea is
really like and what surprised me. Okay,
the food. This part could be very
controversial. The Korean dining scene
can be amazing. It's varied, delicious,
and often very healthy with lots of
vegetables and fermented foods like
kimchi. I generally think that Korean
food can be tastier than any other
cuisine in the world, including
Japanese. But reality needs a closer
look. While home-cooked versions and
freshly made can be great, a lot of the
ones hyped on social media can actually
be very unhealthy. Food can be loaded
with vegetable oils, salt, sugar, and
processed ingredients. Think of going to
the convenience store and grabbing
kimbap and ramen for your lunch. Now,
okay, obviously ramen is not healthy.
But kimbap, it's just rice and
vegetables, right? Then you read the
ingredient list. 20 items long, full of
preservatives and additives you can't
even pronounce. Just like any other
country in the world, eating in Korea
often means avoiding the fast, the
processed, and the cheap. In other
words, those Instagram and Tik Tok
videos you see on food, whether it's a
convenience store, street stall, or
restaurant are often quite unhealthy.
And look, I'm not a saint. Kimbap and
ramen was often my favorite lunch or
dinner. And I had many a John just happy
about how delicious it tasted.
But I also know how I felt afterwards
when they were soggy and really greasy
and I just felt bloated and sick.
Now, I'm somebody that loves meat, but I
still find myself questioning the animal
welfare in South Korea. When you see how
much pork is on the menu, it's hard not
to think about the ethics when pigs are
industrial farmed, kept in cages, and
may not even see the sun. You can watch
the Korean film Okcha if you want the
emotional movie version. And on a
lighter note, the international food
scene can still be quite a challenge.
While some cuisines have improved
massively like Italian thanks culinary
class wars, other options can be few and
far between. If you're looking for an
amazing taco or your favorite South
Indian curry, you might struggle. So if
you're someone that just wants home
comforts or a varied international diet,
you might find yourself missing LA,
London, Sydney, or wherever home is for
you. So what are my suggestions to
satisfy those cravings and stay healthy?
Most importantly, learn how to cook at
home so you know exactly what's going in
your food and you can create any dish
you want. Whether it's a tasty curry or
a luxurious French duck dish and when
you do go out to eat, try and prioritize
those higher quality restaurants and
dishes. It's not about restricting
yourself. It's about prioritizing your
longerterm health and wellbeing. And
look, if you still want that ramen and
kebab, go for it guiltfree. Once you've
sorted out your meals, there's one final
piece of the puzzle. Work. Is it intense
as they say or is there a work life
balance? Let's explore the professional
reality of South Korea. The motto, work
hard, play hard, couldn't be more true.
There's a lot of stress and pressure to
achieve results and work long hours,
even with the 52-hour weekly cap in
place. It's not uncommon to work past
6:00 p.m. or even 8:00 p.m. when hitting
deadlines. This can spill over into the
rest of your life, making it hard to
maintain healthy habits and hobbies, as
well as nurturing relationships with
your friends and family. Sometimes that
can mean canceling dinner with a friend
or loved one just to grind out more
hours in office. And this work life
balance can get even worse with weik.
Though less common nowadays, the culture
of weic company dinners with heavy
drinking still exists. It's not just a
social event. It's seen as extension of
the job itself. And this can make it
hard to say no to an invitation. And
even if you're there, saying no to more
shots of soju. Once one of my managers
got so drunk at a paycheek, he fell
over, hit his head, had to go to
hospital, and end up with stitches. Was
it worth it? Probably not. It can also
be a real shock. The system dealing with
Korean managers and culture, as it can
be extremely hierarchical, sometimes you
just have to do what your boss says
without questioning it. Just do it is a
great motto. It's also unfortunately not
uncommon to face verbal abuse in high
pressure environments. While it's less
common for foreigners, you definitely
can be shouted at or harshly criticized.
It's happened to me. It's not pleasant,
but at the same time, it's just kind of
normal. So, how can you thrive or at
least survive in these kind of
environments? Well, it puts a lot of
ownership on you. First, as much as
possible, try to cultivate healthy
habits like sleeping well and exercising
to deal with stress. Maintain those
relationship with friends and family and
try to find a job that suits you. Maybe
with an international firm that's a bit
more flexible or finding a really good
boss that just understands you. Of
course, all of these are easier said
than done. I get it. But for me, what
really worked was spending less time
drinking after work and more time
exercising, going for walks, and being
mindful. Phew. That's unfiltered side of
working in Korea. But don't let me put
you off. Let's wrap this up. And while
it's still a great idea to move to South
Korea, and I'd love to hear what you
think. So, have I scared you off moving
to Korea? There's a quote I really like,
which is when you travel, you bring
yourself with you. It means that
traveling and moving country is not a
shortcut to happiness. In fact, it's
often more challenging than if you just
stayed at home. However, it's also an
amazing opportunity to grow and
experience the world. It's to build
yourself a new life that sits somewhere
between your home experience and the
culture that you've adopted. Navigating
the complexity, the daily struggles, and
eventual growth. That's what makes it so
worthwhile. And there's no doubt that
Korea is an amazing country with its
rich history and culture, the wonderful
people, and the delicious, varied food.
So, if you've lived abroad, what's a
reality check that you've experienced?
And if you disagree with anything I
